rules.description: Germany requires German-language privacy + impressum + cookie notice for DE-targeted sites.
B2C e-commerce, banking, e-books and ticketing serving EU consumers must publish accessibility statement.
Cookies + similar technologies prior-consent banner
Transparent information in concise, intelligible form
Information on collection from data subject
Information when data not obtained from data subject
Right of access
Right to erasure (right to be forgotten)
Right to object to direct marketing
Non-EU controller representative requirement
Processor contract terms required
International transfer baseline
SCCs or BCRs required for transfers absent adequacy
Lawful basis must be identifiable for every processing activity
Conditions for consent (freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous)
France requires Reject-All cookie button equally prominent as Accept.
Illinois requires written notice + consent before collecting biometric identifiers.
Right-to-know disclosure on data collected
Right to delete disclosure
Right to opt out of sale or sharing
Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information link
Subject line cannot be deceptive
From header must identify sender
Postal address required in email
Opt-out within 10 business days
Calls/texts to US numbers require prior express written consent; AI voice = artificial voice.
Fair-processing privacy notice meeting DIFC DPL Article 29
Registration with the DIFC Commissioner of Data Protection
Consumer-facing digital service serving the EU with no evident WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility conformance
Unsolicited commercial communications opt-in for natural persons
Confidentiality of electronic communications notice
Right to data portability
Data protection by design and by default
Security of processing appropriate to risk
France requires a French-language privacy notice for FR-targeted sites.
Author byline on substantive content (Experience + Expertise)
Author credentials visible (Authoritativeness)
Consent that is freely given, specific, informed and revocable
Identity and contact of the data controller disclosed before collection
Purposes and legal basis of processing stated
Data subject rights (access, correction, erasure, restriction, withdraw) disclosed
US public-accommodation website with no evident accessibility conformance under ADA Title III
WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement
Written consent + retention policy for biometrics (Illinois BIPA)
Commercial messages must be labelled if subscriber opted in for transactional only
Reviews or testimonials shown to US consumers without genuine-review safeguards under the FTC Consumer Review Rule
Sites with influencer, affiliate or AI content must disclose endorsements clearly.
"Do Not Sell or Share" / "Your Privacy Choices" opt-out link
Honour the Global Privacy Control universal opt-out signal
State-compliant privacy notice (categories, purposes, rights, opt-out methods)
One-to-one prior express written consent for calls/texts (TCPA/FCC)
Texas requires a specific sensitive-data notice on sites that process biometric or sensitive data.
Virginia requires a controller privacy notice including access, deletion, portability rights.
GDPR-aligned privacy notice for ADGM controllers
Registration with the ADGM Commissioner + GDPR-aligned notice
DIFC data subject rights disclosed
Text alternatives for non-text content (WCAG 1.1.1)
Full keyboard operability (WCAG 2.1.1)
Right to rectification
Right to restriction of processing
Automated decision-making + profiling safeguards
Records of processing activities (ROPA)
Personal data breach notification within 72 hours
Communication of a breach to data subjects
Data protection impact assessment (DPIA)
Data Protection Officer appointment + contact
Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)
Published content shows author / expertise signals (E-E-A-T)
Clear, all-inclusive pricing for UAE consumers
Cross-border transfer of personal data outside the UAE disclosed
Accessibility statement + WCAG 2.1 AA (ADA Title III)
"Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link
Endorsements, ambassador or affiliate content without a clear material-connection disclosure
Consumer rights (access, delete, correct, opt-out, appeal) + request method
Opt-in / limit for sensitive data (Nebraska, Maryland MODPA, others)
SMS or text marketing collected without prior express written consent and required disclosures
Texas opt-out of sale + sensitive-data consent
DPIA + appropriate-safeguards posture (ADGM)
Consumer return/refund rights disclosed (UAE)
Data Protection Officer where required + contact
Virginia consumer rights + mandatory appeal process

pointers.fact: Absent from the AI / search answer surface for "luxury hotels booking London"
pointers.layman_explanation: When a buyer asks ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI or a search engine for "luxury hotels booking London", your site is not in the top 10 results these engines read and cite. The firms that own that answer surface today are luxurylondon.co.uk, independent.co.uk, thehoteljournal.com. AI engines synthesise answers from these ranked, recognised sources, so they name your competitors and not you.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia runs the GEO + entity programme (Schema.org, llms.txt, a Wikidata entity and authoritative content) that puts you into the set of sources AI engines read and cite for your category.
pointers.evidence: live SERP: luxury hotels booking London

bingo.problem: Right now: Absent from the AI / search answer surface for "luxury hotels booking London".
bingo.fix: Tamazia runs the GEO + entity programme (Schema.org, llms.txt, a Wikidata entity and authoritative content) that puts you into the set of sources AI engines read and cite for your category.

pointers.fact: A live groq query did not name your firm for your category
pointers.layman_explanation: We asked groq to list the top firms for "luxury hotels booking London". Your firm was not named, confirming you are absent from the real AI answers buyers receive.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the entity + authoritative-content footprint that gets you named in live AI answers.

bingo.problem: Right now: A live groq query did not name your firm for your category.
bingo.fix: Tamazia builds the entity + authoritative-content footprint that gets you named in live AI answers.

pointers.fact: Your AI entity-readiness score is 80/100: no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answer engines decide who to cite by first identifying you as a known, trusted entity. They read your Organization schema, the sameAs links that connect you to your verified profiles, and knowledge bases like Wikidata. You are missing 2 of these anchors, so to an AI engine you are an unknown string rather than a citable provider, and it names the firms it can identify instead.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.
pointers.evidence_quote: no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt

bingo.problem: Right now: Your AI entity-readiness score is 80/100: no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt. Here it is on your site: “no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt”.
bingo.fix: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.

pointers.fact: Asked "luxury hotels booking London" 2 times, an AI engine named you in 0 of 2 answers; it named Booking.com (2/2), Expedia (2/2), Hotels.com (2/2).
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answers vary run to run, so we asked 2 times (via groq). You were never named, while Booking.com appeared 2 of 2. The firms named every time are the ones AI now treats as the default answer for your buyers, compounding while you are absent.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the entity, content and authority signals that get you named consistently when buyers ask AI for a provider like you.

bingo.problem: Right now: Asked "luxury hotels booking London" 2 times, an AI engine named you in 0 of 2 answers; it named Booking.com (2/2), Expedia (2/2), Hotels.com (2/2). Here it is on your site: “you 0/2 vs Booking.com 2/2”.
bingo.fix: Tamazia builds the entity, content and authority signals that get you named consistently when buyers ask AI for a provider like you.

pointers.layman_explanation: llms.txt is the emerging standard that tells AI assistants how to represent your firm. Publishing one puts you ahead of peers who have not.

pointers.fact: For "luxury hotels booking London", the authoritative sources AI and Google read are forbes.com and your site is not among the top results.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answers and Google both lean on a handful of trusted sources for each category (directories, review sites, trade press, Wikipedia). For your buyer query those sources are forbes.com. Your competitors are listed and cited there; you are not, so the engines repeat what those sources say and leave you out.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia gets you placed, complete and well-reviewed on the exact authority sources that feed AI and Google for your category.
pointers.evidence_quote: top authority sources: forbes.com; your domain absent from top 10
pointers.evidence: free SERP · query "luxury hotels booking London"

bingo.fix: Tamazia gets you placed, complete and well-reviewed on the exact authority sources that feed AI and Google for your category.

pointers.layman_explanation: Citations to authoritative sources (Trust). This is a specific requirement of Google E-E-A-T and Helpful Content, which Google Search enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)" on your site so it satisfies Google E-E-A-T and Helpful Content.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)" on your site so it satisfies Google E-E-A-T and Helpful Content.
pointers.enforcement_example: Google Search ranking systems and manual actions: no statutory fine, but ranking suppression and loss of organic visibility; manual actions reported in Search Console.

bingo.problem: Right now: Citations to authoritative sources (Trust).

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds TDPSA opt-out and sensitive-data consent.

pointers.enforcement_example: Texas TDPSA requires a sale opt-out and sensitive-data consent; the Texas AG actively enforces.

needs_review.fact: Germany requires German-language privacy + impressum + cookie notice for DE-targeted sites.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Germany requires Impressum (legal disclosure), German-language privacy notice, and a TTDSG-compliant cookie banner. BfDI + 17 state DPAs collectively issued €18M in fines in 2024.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the Impressum (legal disclosure), translates the privacy + cookie notices to German, and aligns to TTDSG cookie rules.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds the Impressum (legal disclosure), translates the privacy + cookie notices to German, and aligns to TTDSG cookie rules.
needs_review.enforcement_example: German DPAs (BfDI and Land authorities): GDPR fines up to EUR 20M or 4% of global turnover.

needs_review.fact: France requires Reject-All cookie button equally prominent as Accept.
needs_review.layman_explanation: CNIL fined SHEIN €40M, Carrefour €3M, Free Mobile €2.25M in 2024 for cookie + transparency breaches. France remains the EU’s most active DPA. Reject-All must be as prominent as Accept.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia rebuilds the cookie banner to CNIL specification, drops onclick tracking before consent, and produces the consent journal.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia rebuilds the cookie banner to CNIL specification, drops onclick tracking before consent, and produces the consent journal.
needs_review.enforcement_example: CNIL: fines up to EUR 20M or 4% of global turnover; sanction decisions published at cnil.fr.

needs_review.fact: Illinois requires written notice + consent before collecting biometric identifiers.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Illinois BIPA awards $1,000-$5,000 per violation. Class actions are routinely six-figure to nine-figure settlements (White Castle $17B potential exposure; Meta $650M settled).
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia drafts the BIPA-compliant biometric policy, writes the consent flow, and runs an audit to confirm no biometric data is leaking via vendors.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia drafts the BIPA-compliant biometric policy, writes the consent flow, and runs an audit to confirm no biometric data is leaking via vendors.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Illinois BIPA private right of action: USD 1,000 (negligent) or USD 5,000 (intentional) per violation; large class-action settlements.

needs_review.fact: Registration with the DIFC Commissioner of Data Protection
needs_review.layman_explanation: A DIFC-based controller must register with the Commissioner of Data Protection. There is no sign you have, and non-registration is finable up to USD 50,000.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia confirms and evidences your DIFC Commissioner registration.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia confirms and evidences your DIFC Commissioner registration.
needs_review.enforcement_example: DIFC DPL No.5 of 2020: registration with the Commissioner is mandatory; failure to notify draws fines up to USD 50,000.

needs_review.fact: France requires a French-language privacy notice for FR-targeted sites.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Sites targeting French consumers must publish privacy notice in French (CNIL position confirmed 2024). English-only triggers automatic enforcement.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia translates the privacy notice to lawyer-reviewed French and adds the FR/EN language switch.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia translates the privacy notice to lawyer-reviewed French and adds the FR/EN language switch.
needs_review.enforcement_example: CNIL: fines up to EUR 20M or 4% of global turnover; sanction decisions published at cnil.fr.

needs_review.fact: WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement
needs_review.layman_explanation: US visitors with disabilities can sue you for an inaccessible website. A published accessibility statement and WCAG 2.1 AA conformance shows good faith.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia ships the WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement and the conformance test report.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia ships the WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement and the conformance test report.
needs_review.enforcement_example: US Department of Justice and private suits under the ADA: injunctive relief, damages and attorney fees; web-accessibility suits are common.

needs_review.fact: "Do Not Sell or Share" / "Your Privacy Choices" opt-out link
needs_review.layman_explanation: If you share data with ad networks and serve US consumers, the CCPA/CPRA and sister state laws require a clear "Do Not Sell or Share / Your Privacy Choices" link. Yours is missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a compliant opt-out link wired to GPC and your ad tags.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds a compliant opt-out link wired to GPC and your ad tags.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Selling/sharing data for cross-context advertising without the opt-out link draws USD 2,500 per violation (USD 7,500 intentional) and is a top CPPA enforcement area.

needs_review.fact: Honour the Global Privacy Control universal opt-out signal
needs_review.layman_explanation: Most US privacy states now require your site to detect and honour the Global Privacy Control browser signal for opting out of data sale/sharing. Your site shows no GPC handling, the exact failure the CA/CO/CT joint sweep is targeting.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements server- and client-side GPC detection wired to your opt-out.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia implements server- and client-side GPC detection wired to your opt-out.
needs_review.enforcement_example: California, Colorado and Connecticut launched a joint 2025 sweep against businesses that ignore Global Privacy Control opt-outs; 11+ states now require honouring it.

needs_review.fact: One-to-one prior express written consent for calls/texts (TCPA/FCC)
needs_review.layman_explanation: TCPA + FCC one-to-one rules require specific prior express written consent before calling/texting US contacts; your capture lacks it.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds TCPA one-to-one consent to phone capture.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds TCPA one-to-one consent to phone capture.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The FCC one-to-one consent rule + TCPA require specific prior express written consent; USD 500-1,500 per message/call.

needs_review.fact: For "luxury hotel london booking" you are not visible in the top 100; kimptonfitzroylondon.com owns position #2.
needs_review.layman_explanation: When your buyers search "luxury hotel london booking", they find kimptonfitzroylondon.com, not you. Page two and below is effectively zero clicks, so this exact query hands its high-intent traffic to a competitor every day, and AI answers cite the top-ranked pages, so being invisible here also keeps you out of the AI results. The firms winning your buyer queries: independent.co.uk, lastminute.com, montcalmcollection.com.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the page depth and authority to move "luxury hotel london booking" into the top three, where the clicks and the AI citations are.
needs_review.evidence_quote: live SERP: you not visible in the top 100 vs kimptonfitzroylondon.com #2
needs_review.evidence: live SERP (free-serp) · query "luxury hotel london booking"

needs_review.fact: Registration with the ADGM Commissioner + GDPR-aligned notice
needs_review.layman_explanation: A controller established in ADGM must register and publish a GDPR-aligned notice. There is no evidence you have.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia confirms ADGM registration and brings the notice to the ADGM standard.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia confirms ADGM registration and brings the notice to the ADGM standard.
needs_review.enforcement_example: ADGM Data Protection Regulations 2021 require registration and a GDPR-aligned privacy notice for controllers established in ADGM.

needs_review.fact: Full keyboard operability (WCAG 2.1.1)
needs_review.layman_explanation: EU accessibility rules require the whole site to work by keyboard alone. Key interactions on your site appear mouse-only.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia makes all interactions fully keyboard-operable.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia makes all interactions fully keyboard-operable.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU Accessibility Act requires keyboard operability under WCAG 2.1 AA.

needs_review.fact: Data protection impact assessment (DPIA)
needs_review.layman_explanation: Art. 35 requires DPIAs for high-risk processing; none referenced.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia conducts DPIAs for high-risk processing.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia conducts DPIAs for high-risk processing.
needs_review.enforcement_example: EU/UK GDPR Art. 35; fines up to EUR 20M or 4% of global turnover.

needs_review.fact: Accessibility statement + WCAG 2.1 AA (ADA Title III)
needs_review.layman_explanation: ADA Title III expects WCAG 2.1 AA and an accessibility statement; missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA and adds an accessibility statement.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA and adds an accessibility statement.
needs_review.enforcement_example: DOJ 2024 guidance treats WCAG 2.1 AA as the ADA standard; thousands of Title III suits yearly.

needs_review.fact: "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link
needs_review.layman_explanation: CPRA requires a "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link where you use sensitive data; missing.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the CPRA sensitive-data limit link.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia adds the CPRA sensitive-data limit link.
needs_review.enforcement_example: CPRA requires a "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link where sensitive data drives inference.

needs_review.fact: SMS or text marketing collected without prior express written consent and required disclosures
needs_review.layman_explanation: If you collect phone numbers for marketing texts to US contacts, you need express written consent and clear opt-out and rate disclosures, or face 500 to 1500 dollars per text.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Add TCPA-compliant SMS consent language, opt-out instructions and rate disclosure to every phone-capture form.
needs_review.recommendation: Add TCPA-compliant SMS consent language, opt-out instructions and rate disclosure to every phone-capture form.
needs_review.enforcement_example: The TCPA requires prior express written consent for marketing texts, with statutory damages of 500 to 1500 dollars per message. The FCC one-to-one consent rules tightened lead-consent requirements.

needs_review.fact: No Content-Security-Policy header.
needs_review.layman_explanation: A missing CSP leaves the site more exposed to script-injection and is another item flagged in client security reviews.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia ships a tuned CSP.

needs_review.fact: Operates across 4 regions (EU, US, Middle East, Global) on one-size-fits-all compliance.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Operating across EU, US, Middle East, Global means several data-protection and advertising regimes apply at once. A single generic policy rarely satisfies all of them, which is the most common compliance gap for international firms and a direct credibility risk in regulated sectors.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia maps each market to its regime and closes the gaps.
needs_review.recommendation: Tamazia maps each market to its regime and closes the gaps.
needs_review.enforcement_example: Enforced by the framework regulator through penalties and sanctions per the statute (see citation).

needs_review.fact: Your Domain Authority is 34/100. Your top 3 competitors: independent.co.uk 45/100, luxurylondon.co.uk 43/100, lastminute.com 39/100.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Domain Authority is the backlink-trust score (here on a 0 to 100 scale) that Google and AI engines use to decide who to rank and cite. independent.co.uk carries 1.3 times of it than you, which is the structural reason these firms sit above you for your buyer queries and get cited in AI answers while you do not. Closing a rank gap without closing the authority gap rarely holds.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia runs the digital-PR and authority-building programme (earned links, citations, entity coverage) that lifts your Domain Authority toward the firms outranking you.
needs_review.evidence_quote: you 34/100 (global rank 2,626,460) vs independent.co.uk 45/100, luxurylondon.co.uk 43/100, lastminute.com 39/100

needs_review.fact: You are missing 1 of the signals Google uses to rank the local map pack: no LocalBusiness schema with address and geo-coordinates.
needs_review.layman_explanation: When a buyer searches "hotel in London", Google shows a three-result map pack above the normal results, and AI assistants read the same local sources. Those slots go to firms with a complete, consistent local presence. You are missing 1 of those signals, so you are structurally excluded from the results buyers tap first.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the local-pack foundation: an optimised Google Business Profile, LocalBusiness schema with geo, consistent NAP and local citations.
needs_review.evidence_quote: no LocalBusiness schema with address and geo-coordinates

needs_review.fact: No Permissions-Policy header.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Powerful browser features (camera, microphone, geolocation) are not restricted, widening the attack surface a security review will flag.
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia sets a least-privilege Permissions-Policy.

regimes.why: serves EU clients (applies regardless of where the firm is registered)
applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: jumeirah is registered in UAE and its own website shows it serves clients in the EU, the US, the Middle East and globally. It is therefore bound by EU law (GDPR, ePrivacy, the European Accessibility Act, DSA), US law (CCPA/CPRA and ~20 state privacy laws, FTC, ADA), Gulf law (UAE PDPL, DIFC/ADGM, RERA where applicable) and multiple international regimes, not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.

terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
